hypothesis: Stocks with extreme short-term RSI readings (below 30 or above 70) tend to revert over the next 3-5 days. This captures short-term overreaction to news and liquidity shocks.
universe: S&P 500 constituents (high liquidity required for short holding period)
entry_logic: Buy when 5-day RSI drops below 20. Short when 5-day RSI exceeds 80. Enter at next open.
exit_logic: Exit when RSI returns to 40-60 neutral zone. Time-based exit after 5 trading days regardless.
position_sizing: Equal weight, max 20 positions at a time. Total exposure capped at 100% long, 50% short.
risk_management: Stop-loss at 3% per position. No holding through earnings announcements. Avoid penny stocks (price > $10, volume > 1M shares/day).
known_failure_modes: Trending markets push RSI to extremes that persist, not revert.
Transaction costs are significant with short holding periods — must be modeled accurately.
Earnings surprises can cause RSI extremes that don't revert.
Slippage in fast-moving stocks at extremes can be material.

historical_performance.notes: The 5-day RSI variant works much better than the traditional 14-day. Performance is sensitive to the entry threshold — requires careful parameter stability testing.

references: Larry Connors - Short Term Trading Strategies That Work
